topic: A student learns their thesis advisor is leaving the university
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the sterile finality of a closing door. Professor Albright was stepping down. My stomach immediately tightened, a cold knot forming just beneath my ribs. I stared at the screen, rereading the carefully worded announcement, searching for some hidden caveat, a sign that this was temporary. But there was only the official resignation notice. I pushed back from my desk, the cheap plastic chair scraping loudly against the linoleum floor. My hands started to tremble slightly as I picked up my coffee mug, the ceramic suddenly feeling slick and foreign in my grip. Every familiar routine—the quiet hum of the library, the comforting weight of his approval in my earlier drafts—suddenly felt fragile, about to shatter. I spent the next hour pacing my small dorm room, the silence amplifying the frantic drumming in my ears, unable to focus on anything but the impending void where my guidance used to be.

[story 2]

"So, he's actually leaving?" I managed to ask, the words sounding thin and reedy even to my own ears. The question hung in the air between me and Sarah, who was meticulously organizing her notes across the table. I watched her, noting the slight hesitation in her movements, the way her gaze kept flicking toward the closed door of the faculty office. A strange pressure built behind my eyes, making the fluorescent lights of the common area seem too harsh, too bright. I tried to focus on the textbook open on my lap, but the equations swam, dissolving into meaningless symbols. I found myself repeatedly tapping my pen against my thigh, a restless, repetitive motion. I needed reassurance, a solid anchor, but the atmosphere in the room felt suddenly thin, like the air before a storm breaks.

[story 3]

I walked into the departmental lounge, deliberately slow, as if navigating a minefield. The air smelled stale, heavy with old paper and lukewarm coffee. When I saw Dr. Albright across the room, he was laughing, a sound that used to feel like a warm blanket, but now seemed distant, echoing in a vast, empty space. My throat constricted. I forced a smile that felt stiff and unnatural, a mask I couldn't quite keep in place. I managed a mumbled greeting, but my voice cracked on the last syllable. I retreated to a secluded corner, sinking onto a worn sofa. I pulled my knees up to my chest, wrapping my arms around myself tightly. The world outside the small circle of my immediate anxiety seemed to vibrate with an unsettling energy, and I couldn't seem to draw a full, steady breath.
